item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was missing the

« SCP-1916 | SCP-1917 | SCP-1918 »

I added it, I hope that was okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I enjoyed this. The entire idea of this skip is enough to entice me, but I felt like the way you broke down what it was and how it acted really showed. Ideas involving the Church can often be a tad bit repetitive if not developed properly, but you fleshed it out enough for clear variation and clearness when put up against similar articles. That's certainly an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Related suggestion: based on the interview log, "does not show any inclination to alter its routine" is misleading. 1917 clearly wants to change what it's doing, it's just in a repeated state of believing that change will come tomorrow.

Changing this description to imply that 1917 is unable to change would give away the game far too early, though, so I would suggest something like "maintains its own routine without the need for outside enforcement", or even "has resisted all efforts to modify its routine" if you're feeling sneaky. (The fact that it doesn't consciously resist can be saved for the reveal in the interview. :P)

Unrelated: I'm amused by how close this entry's number is to both SCP-1914 and SCP-1915.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: It's powered by a steam engine, so surely that implies it actually does need water?

I assume a bit of non-Euclidean cooling areas can make that a closed cycle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As SCP-1917 is capable of providing all components necessary to its survival, and does not show any inclination to alter its routine, it requires no human interaction to contain.

Last comma should be a period, or at least a semicolon.

Edit: sorry, misread it, that comma is alright. The first comma should be removed though. It is misplaced, and I think it broke the flow of the sentence enough for me to misread it :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this. Just a sad robot man who's planning on quitting and doesn't realise he's already been prevented from doing so. Kind of reminds me of my one big fear in a subdued kind of way.

I have a couple of questions, primarily stemming from this line:

The entity does not require either food or water, and has shown to be capable of surviving in highly toxic, low-oxygen environments.

1: It's powered by a steam engine, so surely that implies it actually does need water?
2: When or how was it shown to have been able to survive toxic environments, if they have never moved it out of its little house?

I noticed a couple of minor issues (a repeated "the" and a missing full stop) which I took the liberty of correcting, hope that's ok.

In any case, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this, but something isn't clicking here.

After I read the interview I was hyped to read something like a transcript of his sermon but when the file just ended, I felt let down a bit. Don't get me wrong, I liked the interview but I was expecting a little more than just that.

Novote for now unless I change my mind later on

item.title: In which a gigantic cyborg copes with memory-loss
item.content:encoded:
Okay. This one. Commentary's been a long time coming. Honestly, looking back, I'm actually really pleased with how it turned out. I think I did the concept justice? Maybe? I'm probably not a good judge.

Thanks to Stallmantic, CadaverCommander, and Drblackbox for pre-posting critique, and credit to Harpsichord23 for the title.
